summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

What is a histogram?

A histogram is a bar graph for continuous data where adjacent bars represent frequency counts for consecutive intervals (bins) with no gaps.

What is a 'normal distribution'?

A normal distribution is the classic bell curve -- symmetric and mound-shaped, with about 68% of data within one standard deviation of the mean.

What is a 'frequency'?

Frequency is the number of times a data value or group of values occurs. In a histogram, the height of each bar represents the frequency for that interval.

What is a 'bin' in a histogram?

A bin is a consecutive interval on the horizontal axis of a histogram. Each bar represents the count of data values that fall within that bin.

What is 'positively skewed' another name for?

Positively skewed and right-skewed are the same thing. The tail of the distribution extends toward larger (more positive) values on the right.

What is 'negatively skewed' another name for?

Negatively skewed and left-skewed are the same thing. The tail extends toward smaller (more negative) values on the left.

summary_sections.title: Real-World Applications
summary_sections.content_html:
Real-World Applications

Example: A city planner collects commute times for 200 residents. The histogram shows a peak around 20-30 minutes with equal tails on both sides. What distribution shape is this?

A single peak with approximately equal tails on both sides describes a symmetric, bell-shaped distribution, like a normal curve.

Example: A college professor finds that most students score between 85 and 95 on an easy exam, with a few students scoring as low as 40-50. What type of skew does this distribution have?

Most data clusters at the high end (85-95) with a tail extending to the left toward low scores. This is a left-skewed (negatively skewed) distribution.

Example: A real estate agent plots home prices in a neighborhood. Most homes are $200,000-$400,000, but a few luxury homes are $1,000,000+. Which is likely true about the mean and median?

The luxury homes create a right-skewed distribution. In right-skewed data, the high values pull the mean above the median.

Example: A teacher creates a histogram of student heights in a class. The histogram is approximately bell-shaped. About what percentage of students fall within one standard deviation of the mean?

For a normal (bell-shaped) distribution, approximately 68% of data falls within one standard deviation of the mean, according to the empirical rule.

Example: A factory measures widget diameters. When they plot a histogram, they see a single peak centered at the target diameter with equal tails. Should the factory manager be concerned about the production process?

A symmetric distribution centered on the target diameter indicates the manufacturing process is producing widgets that consistently cluster around the desired size.

summary_sections.title: Special Topics
summary_sections.content_html:
Important Concepts

In a right-skewed distribution, the tail extends to the:

A right-skewed (positively skewed) distribution has a longer tail that extends to the right, with most data clustered on the left.

In a left-skewed distribution, which is typically true

In a left-skewed distribution, the tail extends to the left, pulling the mean lower than the median.

In a right-skewed distribution, which relationship is typically true

In a right-skewed distribution, the long right tail pulls the mean to the right, making the mean greater than the median.

What does 'skewed' mean when describing a data distribution

A skewed distribution is asymmetric, with data bunched on one side and a longer tail stretching in the other direction.

What is a 'frequency'

Frequency is the number of times a data value or group of values occurs. In a histogram, the height of each bar represents the frequency for that interval.

What is 'positively skewed' another name for

Positively skewed and right-skewed are the same thing. The tail of the distribution extends toward larger (more positive) values on the right.

What is 'negatively skewed' another name for

Negatively skewed and left-skewed are the same thing. The tail extends toward smaller (more negative) values on the left.

A survey of household incomes in a small town produces a right-skewed histogram. A journalist reports the median income as $45,000. What can you infer about the mean income

In a right-skewed distribution, high values in the right tail pull the mean above the median. Therefore the mean income is likely greater than the $45,000 median.
